Center: US National Nuclear Data Center, Brookhaven, USA
title: Excitation functions of proton-induced nuclear reactions on titanium

x4freetext: Excitation functions of proton-induced nuclear
reactions on titanium

x4codes.hlp: Inst. Nucl. Science & Technology, AERE, Savar, Dhaka, Bangladesh
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Berkeley, CA, United States of America

x4freetext: The activities of the radionuclides formed in
the titanium target samples were measured by using a
HPGe gamma-ray detector associated with the necessary
electronics and Maestro data acquisition software in
the spectroscopy facilities at FZJ, Germany, and LBNL,
USA. The energy resolutions (FWHM) at 1332.5 keV of
60Co of the HPGe detectors used were 1.9 keV at FZJ and
2.5 keV at LBNL.
The 25 microm thick Ti foils of natural isotopic
composition consisting of five stable isotopes,
49Ti (5.41 %) and 50Ti (5.18 %) (supplied by
Goodfellow; purity: 99.6 %) were cut in circular
discs with a diameter of 13 mm. These foils were also
used as beam monitors. Six natTi-containing stacks
were irradiated with protons.
Excitation functions of the proton-
induced reactions on natural titanium were measured
by the stacked-sample activation technique. The
counting was done repeatedly in several time segments
depending on the half-life of the radionuclide.
Measurements were carried out at distances of 10 cm,
20 cm and 30 cm from the surface of the detector to
keep the detector dead time below 5 %. At these
counting positions, the effect of extended
sample-size on the efficiency, and the random and
true coincidence losses were negligible.
The uncertainty in the measured cross section
value was determined from the uncertainties
associated with measurements: peak area (0.2-8 %),
efficiency of the detector (4 %), gamma-ray intensities
(0.0001-1.2 %), half-life (0.02-0.3 %) and proton flux
(6 %). The overall uncertainty of measured cross
sections amounted to 7-14 % (1 sigma). Due to weak bad
shape peak at 372.76 keV emitted in the decay of 43Sc,
a large uncertainty was associated in the peak area
analysis particularly in lower energy region (<16 MeV)
as well as with its measured cross section.
Peak area.
Efficiency of the detector.
Gamma-ray intensities.
Half-life.
Proton flux.
The count rate of each product radionuclide was
extrapolated to the end of bombardment (EOB) and
thereafter converted to decay rate by introducing the
necessary corrections for the efficiency of the
detector and the intensity of the gamma-ray.
BP
44.5+-0.2 MeV primary beam protons at JULIC at FZJ.
